definition: The regime where both continuation (rising debt, potential doom loop) and correction (private-sector recession via sectoral balance constraint) are economically damaging. This paradox is the core finding of the research program: it explains why the regime persists and why financial repression emerges as the path of least resistance, being the option that avoids both acute crisis modes.
evidence: NK framework identifies doom loop as tail risk from fiscal continuation
PK framework identifies balance-sheet recession from consolidation (100% base rate)
Pair_2 verdict: PK framework 'substantially stronger' on consolidation risk
Both risks corroborated independently across multiple debate pairs
The paradox not either tail risk individually is why the regime persists
open_questions: Is there a consolidation path narrow enough to avoid recession but wide enough to stabilize debt/GDP?
Can monetary and FX offsets widen the feasible consolidation corridor?
What role could immigration or productivity growth play in resolving the paradox?
Is the paradox itself evidence that financial repression is already the implicit policy?
